summary: Joel describes debugging CSS rendering issues on his redesigned blog, particularly with IE6's unpredictable behavior. He details fixing RSS feed display problems by inlining styles, and notes that image pixelation issues are caused by IE's poor image scaling compared to Firefox and Safari. The post is a casual, lighthearted update mixing technical gripes with office culture.
key_points: IE6 CSS rendering bugs are so unpredictable that random mutations to stylesheets are the most practical debugging approach
RSS readers aggressively strip CSS, forcing developers to inline styles for proper rendering
Browser quality directly affects user experience—Firefox and Safari handle image scaling far better than IE
money_quotes: The best way to fix CSS problems with IE6 is to generate random mutations on the style sheet until it looks fixed.
CSS is nondeterministic, and many better minds than mine have gone completely stark raving mad trying to understand the rhyme and reason of IE6 rendering bugs.
If you use a better browser, you get better results.
key_insight: IE6's CSS rendering was so broken that debugging it was effectively a random process, and browser choice materially affected the quality of the web experience.
